us-politics.headline: Trump's Iran ultimatum raises war fears and legal questions
us-politics.body:
Trump has issued a fresh deadline to Iran over its nuclear program, with Fox News reporting the administration views negotiations as nearing a conclusion. A US military rescue mission for a missing service member resulted in an aircraft being destroyed, with Iran claiming responsibility for the damage. The operation adds friction to already tense diplomacy.

Critics are pushing back hard. Sen. Chris Murphy called Trump's threats against Iran 'a clear war crime,' while NPR reports Trump faces a narrow political window - hawkish enough to satisfy his base, but wary of a prolonged conflict with no clear exit. The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly a fifth of global oil flows, sits at the center of any military calculus. Netanyahu separately confirmed a call with Trump, praising cooperation on the pilot rescue.

sources.title: Why the Strait of Hormuz matters as Trump issues fresh ultimatum to Iran
Iran war nears ‘completion’ as Trump eyes deadline — what the endgame could look like
As Trump ramps up his Iran war messaging, he remains in a tight spot politically
Chris Murphy on Trump’s Iran threat: ‘A clear war crime’
Netanyahu says he spoke with Trump, congratulated him on rescuing downed pilot
Iran says US aircraft destroyed during recovery mission for missing service member

us-politics.headline: GOP scrambles on ICE funding as Republican divisions deepen
us-politics.body:
House Republicans are racing to pass a standalone bill boosting ICE and Border Patrol funding, but the push is straining the broader reconciliation agenda. Leadership wants the win on immigration enforcement - a core Trump priority - but members disagree on whether to move it separately or fold it into the larger spending package. The clock is tight.

The Hill reports the funding gambit is squeezing other Republican priorities, with fiscal hawks and border hardliners pulling in different directions. Fox News frames the effort as urgent and necessary, citing border security as a mandate from voters. Democrats have largely stayed out of the internal fight, watching Republicans manage the tension between ambition and arithmetic.

sources.title: GOP’s ICE funding gambit squeezes Republican agenda
GOP races to pass ICE, Border Patrol funding bill as priorities pile up, divisions emerge

us-politics.headline: Trump health secrecy, mail-in voting order draw sharp criticism
us-politics.body:
The Washington Post reports that the Trump administration is conducting global health negotiations with unusual secrecy, alarming foreign governments and public health experts who say the lack of transparency risks undermining international cooperation. Details of the deals remain undisclosed even to some US officials.

Separately, Stacey Abrams called Trump's executive order restricting mail-in voting 'patently illegal,' arguing it oversteps presidential authority and targets Democratic-leaning voters. The order is likely to face legal challenges. Two Democratic lawmakers also drew attention after visiting Cuba and criticizing US fuel restrictions as an 'economic bombing' - comments that Fox News highlighted as out of step with mainstream Democratic positioning ahead of midterm primaries.

sources.title: Trump administration’s secrecy on health deals alarms experts, governments
Pair of Democrat lawmakers slam 'blockade of fuel' to Cuba, 'economic bombing' after visit to island
Stacey Abrams on Trump’s mail-in voting order: ‘Patently illegal’

us-politics.headline: Michigan primary, Virginia polls signal turbulent midterm terrain
us-politics.body:
The Michigan Democratic Senate primary is heating up after progressive streamer Hasan Piker appeared at a rally for candidate Abdul El-Sayed, triggering backlash over Piker's past comments. The controversy is testing how far left the party's primary electorate will go in a competitive state Democrats need to hold.

In Virginia, a new Washington Post poll shows Governor Abigail Spanberger deeply divided in approval ratings just months after winning by a wide margin. Affordability concerns are driving dissatisfaction among voters who backed her. Fox News notes several high-stakes primaries this month will offer the first real read on whether Trump's second term has shifted the political map - or simply hardened existing lines.

sources.title: Hasan Piker controversy heats up Michigan Senate primary for Democrats
Primary pause, political firestorm: High-stakes elections this month take center stage
Virginians deeply divided on Spanberger months after her landslide win

au-politics.headline: Australia's Fuel Crisis Bites Hard Over Easter Long Weekend
au-politics.body:
Diesel prices have surged again despite Labor's fuel tax relief measures, leaving outback communities and regional businesses exposed. An iconic outback pub near the Oodnadatta Track recorded just two visitors over Easter, with the owner directly blaming fuel costs for keeping tourists away. The crisis is hitting hardest in remote areas where there are no alternatives.

Critics on the right argue the government has failed to secure Australia's fuel supply, pointing to the United States as a model for strategic oil reserves. The Guardian's live price tracker shows ongoing volatility across petrol and diesel, with no clear stabilisation in sight. The political pressure on Labor is building from multiple directions.

sources.title: Diesel remains volatile as prices rise again despite Labor’s fuel tax relief
Outback tourist pub had two visitors this Easter amid fuel price pinch
Track current petrol and diesel prices, service station outages and shipments – Australia's fuel crisis in charts
The US guaranteed its oil, why can’t Australia?

au-politics.headline: Cyclone Maila Threatens Far North Queensland After Narelle's Devastation
au-politics.body:
Severe Tropical Cyclone Maila is tracking toward far north Queensland, arriving just three weeks after Cyclone Narelle tore through the same region. Communities already dealing with damaged infrastructure and recovery efforts now face a second major weather event in rapid succession. Emergency services are preparing for another significant impact.

Separately, a man's body was recovered after he was swept away in outback floodwaters near Innamincka, underscoring the deadly reach of extreme weather events across the country this season.

sources.title: Severe Tropical Cyclone Maila on track to hit far north Queensland three weeks after Narelle tore through
Man’s body found after being swept away in outback flood waters

au-politics.headline: Hanson Preferences and Jury Trials Stir Conservative Debate
au-politics.body:
The Spectator has run a cluster of pieces reflecting conservative anxieties ahead of the federal election. One argues Pauline Hanson's One Nation is surging in preference flows, positioning the party as a significant force in determining the next government. Another warns that proposed changes to jury trial laws represent a serious erosion of civil liberties.

A separate piece draws a pointed historical parallel, cautioning that the Liberal Party risks the same collapse that destroyed Lloyd George's British Liberals. Taken together, the commentary signals a right-of-centre debate about whether the Coalition can hold its base or whether votes are bleeding to minor parties.

sources.title: Pauline Hanson: straight to number one
The Tories should beware the fate of Lloyd George’s Liberals
Scrapping jury trials will cost us all

au-politics.headline: Chinese AI, Iran Conflict and Artemis Moon Mission Dominate Global Coverage
au-politics.body:
ABC reporting reveals Chinese artificial intelligence is allegedly helping Iran target US forces with greater precision in the Middle East, raising serious questions about technology transfer and the role of Beijing in regional conflicts. A separate ABC explainer details how US forces used a deception campaign to rescue a downed fighter pilot from Iranian territory.

On a different frontier, the Artemis II mission is closing in on the farthest point humans have travelled from Earth, with the crew preparing to orbit the Moon. The mission marks a significant milestone in NASA's return to deep space exploration.

sources.title: Chinese AI helping Iran target US forces with 'incredible precision'
Deception campaign key to rescue of downed US fighter pilot from Iran
Artemis II chases record for the farthest humans have ventured from Earth

world.headline: US-Israeli Strikes on Iran Enter Day 38, Killing Dozens
world.body:
American and Israeli forces bombed Iran's top university on Sunday, killing 34 people, as the 38-day campaign shows no sign of slowing. Tehran has rejected a Trump deadline to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, raising fears of a broader economic chokehold on global oil flows. Trump separately claimed the US armed Iranian dissidents through Kurdish intermediaries - a charge Kurdish groups flatly denied.

The war's ripple effects are spreading fast. Yemen's Houthis have entered the conflict on Iran's side, leaving Yemeni civilians bracing for heavier retaliation. In Vietnam, gig workers are being squeezed by fuel price spikes tied directly to the fighting. Analysts are already debating what a reshaped regional order around the Strait of Hormuz might look like.

sources.title: Yemen’s civilians fear fallout after Houthis enter Iran war
Trump says US armed Iranian dissidents via Kurds, Kurdish groups deny claim
Iran’s top university bombed as US, Israel intensify attacks; 34 killed
Iran war: What is happening on day 38 of US-Israeli attacks?
A new regional order for the Strait of Hormuz
Vietnam’s gig workers slammed by rising fuel costs amid fallout of Iran war
Trump declares victory after rescue but threats to US operation in Iran still loom
Iran war live: Tehran rejects Trump’s Hormuz deadline; 2 killed in Haifa

world.headline: Israel Displaces Over a Million as Lebanon Offensive Intensifies
world.body:
Israeli airstrikes have forced up to 1.2 million people from their homes across Lebanon, with families streaming into mountain villages and makeshift shelters to escape the bombardment. Entire communities have been uprooted, and aid workers warn that displacement on this scale is straining Lebanon's already fragile infrastructure.

Reports from the ground describe villages reduced to rubble and families with nowhere permanent to go. The crisis is deepening a humanitarian emergency in a country still recovering from years of economic collapse.

sources.title: Up to 1.2 million people forced to flee as Israel pummels Lebanon
Families fleeing Israeli attacks take refuge in Lebanon’s mountains
Displaced in Lebanon: 'Lives turned upside down'

world.headline: Iraq Qualifies for World Cup for First Time in Decades
world.body:
Iraq has secured a place at the 2026 World Cup, ending a long absence from football's biggest stage. Australian coach Graham Arnold, who guided the team to qualification, received a hero's welcome both in Baghdad and in Sydney, where the Iraqi diaspora turned out in force to celebrate.

The achievement carries weight well beyond sport in a country that has endured decades of conflict and instability. Scenes of jubilation across Iraq underscored how much the qualification meant to ordinary Iraqis.

sources.title: World Cup qualification earns Iraq coach Arnold a hero’s welcome in Sydney
Iraqis give rousing welcome to coach behind historic World Cup return

world.headline: Nigeria Church Attack, Bangladesh Measles Surge, and Artemis II
world.body:
Nigerian army forces rescued 31 people taken hostage following an Easter Sunday attack on a church, in the latest episode of violence targeting Christian communities in the country's volatile regions. No group has yet claimed responsibility.

In Bangladesh, a deadly measles spike is alarming health officials, driven by gaps in vaccination coverage that widened during the COVID-19 pandemic. Separately, NASA's Artemis II mission is drawing closer, with a new visual breakdown of the crewed lunar flyby highlighting how far the programme has advanced since the Apollo era.

sources.title: Why Bangladesh is seeing a deadly spike in measles
Nigerian army rescues 31 held after Easter church attack
A visual guide to Artemis II and previous missions to the moon

ai.headline: Gemma 4 Goes Local: On iPhones, Laptops, and Browsers
ai.body:
Google's Gemma 4 model is landing on consumer hardware fast. It's running on iPhones via the Google AI Edge Gallery app, inside browsers without API keys through projects like Gemma Gem, and on M3 Pro MacBooks doing real-time audio and video processing. Developers are also spinning it up locally through LM Studio's new headless CLI alongside Claude Code.

The flurry of community projects signals a shift in how people are deploying capable models - away from cloud APIs and toward local inference. The hardware is now good enough that a modern laptop or phone can run a useful model without phoning home. That changes the privacy calculus and the cost structure for a lot of applications.

sources.title: Show HN: Gemma Gem – AI model embedded in a browser – no API keys, no cloud
Gemma 4 on iPhone
Show HN: Real-time AI (audio/video in, voice out) on an M3 Pro with Gemma E2B
Running Gemma 4 locally with LM Studio's new headless CLI and Claude Code

ai.headline: Microsoft Calls Copilot 'Entertainment Only' in Its Own Terms
ai.body:
Microsoft's terms of service now describe Copilot as being "for entertainment purposes only" - a remarkable disclaimer for a product the company has aggressively marketed as a productivity tool and embedded across its enterprise software stack. The language creates a stark gap between the sales pitch and the legal fine print.

The framing likely reflects liability concerns, but it hands critics a sharp line of attack. If Microsoft won't legally stand behind Copilot's outputs, businesses relying on it for real work should probably notice. It also fits a broader pattern: AI companies racing to deploy products while quietly hedging on accountability in the terms nobody reads.

sources.title: Copilot is ‘for entertainment purposes only,’ according to Microsoft’s terms of use

ai.headline: AI and Copyright Collide: Suno's Music Problem Gets Messy
ai.body:
Suno, the AI music generation startup, is deepening its collision with copyright law. The Verge reports the service is producing outputs that closely mirror existing recordings, raising questions the company has so far avoided answering cleanly. The music industry's lawsuits against AI generators are still working through courts, but the evidence keeps accumulating.

Separately, the media scraper gallery-dl is migrating from GitHub to Codeberg after receiving a DMCA notice - a smaller but telling sign of how copyright enforcement is reshaping where open-source tools can live. Both cases point to the same pressure: rights holders are getting more aggressive, and platforms are responding by pushing projects out rather than fighting.

sources.title: Media scraper Gallery-dl is moving to Codeberg after receiving a DMCA notice
Suno is a music copyright nightmare

ai.headline: LLMs Are Changing How Developers Architect Software
ai.body:
A widely discussed post argues that coding with LLMs nudges developers toward microservices - not because microservices are better, but because LLMs handle small, self-contained codebases more reliably than large monoliths. The model's context window becomes an architectural constraint.

Meanwhile, a developer built a tiny LLM from scratch to demystify how language models actually work, and another shipped Modo, an open-source alternative to AI coding tools like Cursor and Windsurf. The pattern across all three is the same: developers are no longer just using AI tools, they're pulling them apart, rebuilding them, and questioning what the tools are quietly optimizing for.

sources.title: Does coding with LLMs mean more microservices?
Show HN: I built a tiny LLM to demystify how language models work
Show HN: Modo – I built an open-source alternative to Kiro, Cursor, and Windsurf

btc.headline: Bitcoin Reclaims $69K as Iran Tensions Ease
btc.body:
Bitcoin climbed back above $69,000 after ceasefire talks between the U.S. and Iran emerged, squeezing short sellers who had bet on further downside. The relief rally came as Trump delayed a deadline tied to Iran, giving risk assets room to breathe. Crypto prediction markets had been tracking the conflict in real time, functioning as a live macro sentiment gauge according to Sygnum.

Despite the bounce, the picture remains fragile. One analyst warns a meltdown to $10,000 is still on the table unless Bitcoin can reclaim $75,000 and hold it. Inflation data this week could be the next catalyst either way. Michael Saylor hinted Strategy is preparing another Bitcoin purchase after a week-long pause, which may signal some institutional confidence returning.

sources.title: First real bull signal since 2025? Five things to know in Bitcoin this week
Iran war bets turn prediction markets into real-time macro radar: Sygnum
Inflation takes center stage: Crypto Week Ahead
Bitcoin reclaims $69,000 as ceasefire talks surface and crypto shorts get squeezed
Bitcoin meltdown to $10,000 remains likely unless prices reclaim $75,000, analyst says
Michael Saylor hints at next Strategy Bitcoin buy after weeklong pause
Trump-Iran deadline chaos sends crypto higher while ceasefire hopes rise

btc.headline: Circle Builds Quantum-Resistant Layer-1 as IMF Flags Tokenization Risk
btc.body:
Circle unveiled a quantum-resistant roadmap for Arc, its new layer-1 blockchain, building post-quantum cryptography into the network from launch rather than retrofitting it later. The move positions Arc ahead of a threat most chains are still ignoring.

The timing is pointed. The IMF this week warned that tokenizing real-world assets could drag crypto's volatility and counterparty risks into mainstream financial markets, potentially destabilizing institutions that adopt it. Circle's quantum focus suggests at least one major issuer is thinking about infrastructure durability, not just short-term adoption. Whether regulators treat that as reassuring or insufficient remains to be seen.

sources.title: IMF warns tokenization could bring crypto risks into global financial markets
Circle future-proofs Arc blockchain against quantum threats
Circle unveils quantum-resistant roadmap for its layer-1 blockchain Arc

btc.headline: China Bans Dorsey's Bitchat, Pushes Blockchain for Banks
btc.body:
Apple pulled Jack Dorsey's Bitchat messaging app from its China App Store at Beijing's request. The app had gained traction among protesters in Iran, making it a target. The removal underscores how quickly decentralization-adjacent tools get caught in geopolitical crossfire.

In a separate move, China's tax authority urged banks to adopt blockchain for lending services - a reminder that Beijing's hostility to open crypto networks coexists with active state-directed blockchain deployment. The two actions together illustrate the same pattern: suppress permissionless tools, promote permissioned ones.

sources.title: China's tax authority urges banks to implement blockchain for lending services
China orders Apple to pull Dorsey's Bitchat, the messaging app used during Iran protests
Apple pulls Jack Dorsey's Bitchat from China at Beijing’s request

btc.headline: North Korea Has Quietly Infiltrated DeFi for Seven Years
btc.body:
North Korean operatives have been embedding themselves inside DeFi protocols since the sector's earliest days in 2019, according to a cybersecurity researcher. The infiltration spans countless projects and goes well beyond the high-profile hacks that make headlines. Workers posed as legitimate developers, giving Pyongyang persistent access to codebases and treasuries.

The scale suggests the problem is structural, not incidental. Many DeFi teams hired pseudonymous contributors with little verification, creating obvious entry points. Rwanda's decision to block Bybit's peer-to-peer franc-to-crypto platform this week adds another data point to a broader story of regulators and bad actors both stress-testing crypto's open edges.

sources.title: North Korean workers have been infiltrating DeFi for 7 years: Researcher
Rwanda swats Bybit’s P2P platform offering franc-to-crypto trading

food.name: Miso-Glazed Aubergine (Nasu Dengaku)
food.description: Halved aubergines roasted until silky and caramelised under a sweet-savoury miso and mirin glaze
food.tip: Score the flesh in a crosshatch before glazing so the miso penetrates deeply rather than just sitting on the surface

exercise.name: Monday Reset: Loaded Carry Circuit
exercise.equipment: Two kettlebells or heavy shopping bags, a clear corridor or outdoor path
exercise.description: Alternate between farmer carries, suitcase carries (one side only), and overhead carries for 40-metre lengths with 90-second rests, building grip strength, core stability, and posture after a weekend of likely sitting

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus Problem and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and when applied to humans, whose cells, memories, and beliefs constantly change, it becomes genuinely unsettling
learn.why: Derek Parfit's modern take in 'Reasons and Persons' pushes this into questions about whether you are the same person who made promises last year, which has surprising implications for how we think about guilt, credit, and long-term goals — a productive rabbit hole for a Monday reset
